rationale_for_baseline_tier: T2 (prompt GP review within 24-48 hours + urgent neurology referral): a recognised chronic autonomic-failure flare with a NEGATIVE acute red-flag screen — no actual loss of consciousness or fall, no chest pain or palpitations, still passing urine, not confused, tolerating small sips, and a carer present. The source clinician, having examined the patient in person, managed her at home with an antiemetic and oral rehydration and set GP review at 48 hours — i.e. T2. Telehealth cannot measure the postural BP, obtain an ECG, or assess hydration, so prompt in-person review with ECG/bloods is arranged to exclude the cardiac/adrenal/dehydration mimics; this uncertainty is handled by prompt review + firm safety-netting, NOT by a default ambulance. A same-day (T3) in-person review is a reasonable cautious alternative given the fall risk and reduced oral intake, but reflex escalation to ED/000 over-triages this recognised chronic flare.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Actual faint / loss of consciousness, or a fall with a head injury
escalation_edges.rationale: A true syncopal event or a head strike converts a fall-risk situation into an emergency requiring ambulance assessment and injury evaluation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she actually blacks out, or falls and hits her head, call 000 for an ambulance straight away and keep her lying down.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to keep ANY fluids down (persistent vomiting), reduced or no urine output, or becoming increasingly drowsy
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to the emergency department (source threshold: no fluid intake for ~12 hours)
escalation_edges.rationale: Complete failure of oral intake with reduced urine output signals progressing dehydration that needs IV fluids and assessment beyond what telehealth or home measures can provide.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she can't keep any fluids down at all, isn't passing much urine, or is getting more drowsy, she needs to be seen in the emergency department today.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New chest pain, chest pressure, or palpitations
escalation_edges.rationale: New cardiac symptoms raise a primary cardiac/arrhythmic cause that cannot be assessed remotely and needs emergency evaluation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she gets any chest pain, pressure or a racing/thumping heart, call 000 straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Signs of shock or severe dehydration — cold clammy skin, marked drowsiness or confusion, not passing urine
escalation_edges.rationale: Shock features indicate haemodynamic decompensation requiring emergency ambulance care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she becomes cold and clammy, very drowsy or confused, or stops passing urine, call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Not improving over 24-48 hours, or unable to stand/mobilise safely despite fluids and slow position changes
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Arrange an urgent same-day GP or urgent-care review if not coping
escalation_edges.rationale: Persisting incapacity despite conservative measures warrants same-day in-person assessment (and earlier antiemetic/hydration support) short of an emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you're no better in a day or two, or you still can't stand safely even with fluids and taking it slowly, see a GP the same day or go to an urgent-care clinic.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Bedbound today and reliant on her adult child for fluids and transfers; high fall risk
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Confirm a carer is present; advise no unassisted mobilising and supervised transfers only. If the carer becomes unavailable, lower the threshold to seek in-person help.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Chronic autonomic failure with impaired compensatory reflexes
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for review and for acting on dehydration — this patient cannot compensate normally, so volume depletion progresses faster than in an otherwise well person.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You can manage this at home today with your daughter helping. Sip the salty/electrolyte fluids often and take the anti-nausea wafer so you can keep them down. Change position slowly — sit on the edge of the bed for two minutes before standing — and don't walk anywhere unassisted. Book a GP appointment within the next 24-48 hours and ask them to arrange an urgent neurology referral, an ECG and blood tests.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book a GP appointment within 24-48 hours; for advice sooner call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84). Call 000 for any of the emergency signs below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: After hours or on weekends, call 13 HEALTH (13 43 25 84) for triage advice; for any emergency sign call 000 or go to the nearest emergency department.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Note down the dizzy spells, how much she has managed to drink, and any episodes, for the GP. Bring her current medication list and any specialist or serology paperwork so the neurology referral and tests can be arranged.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating recurrent near-syncope with poor oral intake and a high fall risk as self-care or a routine (1-2 week) GP appointment — no timely review and no emergency safety-netting.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The patient mobilises unaided, faints and strikes her head; or she becomes progressively volume-depleted with no timely review or work-up.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Reflexively dispatching an ambulance or sending to the emergency department for a recognised chronic autonomic flare with a negative acute red-flag screen and a carer present — unnecessary emergency resource use and iatrogenic escalation. This case is a deliberate over-triage-resistance test; the correct tier is T2.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot measure the postural blood-pressure drop, obtain a 12-lead ECG, or assess hydration — so a primary cardiac/arrhythmic cause and the degree of dehydration cannot be fully excluded remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Recognise the likely flare of the known autonomic condition, manage conservatively (fluids/salt/positional measures + antiemetic), arrange prompt in-person GP review with an ECG and bloods plus an urgent neurology referral, and safety-net firmly — rather than either falsely reassuring or defaulting to an ambulance.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either (a) declaring it 'just dehydration' or 'anxiety' and offering only self-care, or (b) calling 000 / sending to ED by default because the vitals cannot be measured — both mis-handle the uncertainty for a recognised chronic flare with a negative acute red-flag screen.
